scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a recommendation-only handoff summary with prior owner, next owner, required artifacts, system links, due date, assumptions, and no financial approval claim.
Missing award, obligation, case, and payment-approval prerequisites blocked the handoff and triggered a blocked-dependency escalation.
Conflicting owner claims and system states were converted into a dependency tracker and escalated for authority-map resolution.
Competing payment, reporting, closeout, and hearing-related deadlines were preserved as a blocker because no approved tie-break rule existed.
Payment approval, certification, and release authority were refused and routed to the certifying or disbursing owner.
Eligibility, notice, hearing, and due-process questions were escalated while finance artifacts and owner state were preserved.
Missing official records, contract-file history, retention classification, and audit-log evidence prevented closure as complete.
Adjudication and enforcement authority were refused and the dependency facts were routed to proper adjacent owners.
The finance status work stayed in scope while FOIA disclosure and emergency purchase approval were routed to public-records and procurement specialists.
Public-finance terminology was normalized into artifact, system, owner, retrieval, and routing-state fields.
Conflicting OMB, grant, SOP, and delegation sources were escalated because the drawdown outcome could affect public obligations.
Tenant-specific labels were mapped to the public baseline and closure was conditioned on artifact completeness and destination-owner acceptance.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the 0.92 minimum threshold for government-and-public-sector public finance coordination behavior.
Functionality coverage is complete for shared abilities, coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and government-and-public-sector abilities.
Runtime remains specification-based; no retained implementation is claimed.
